MIT 6.C85 / 11.C25 — Vis & Society · Assignment 2

Zoning as a Sorting Machine

Exploratory Data Analysis · Subtheme: Zoning and Housing Supply

Ching Lam Choi: chinglam@mit.edu
  • Theme & Data
  • Questions
  • Data Overview

  • Q2

  • Summary
01 — Chosen Subtheme

Zoning and Housing Supply

I chose Subtheme 2: Zoning and Housing Supply. Zoning rules determine where and what kinds of housing can be built, and decades of research show that exclusionary single-family zoning is one of the most durable mechanisms by which American cities reproduce spatial inequality. Greater Boston is a sharp case: one of the most expensive housing markets in the country, yet fragmented across 101 municipalities each with their own zoning codes.

Overarching Thesis

Zoning does not just shape buildings, it shapes who gets to live where. This analysis asks whether Metro Boston's land-use rules function as a sorting machine that stratifies residents by income and race, and whether the gains from loosening zoning near transit accrue to residents or to developers.

Question 1

The Landscape of Exclusion

Where is zoning most restrictive, and does it correlate with income and racial composition?

Question 2

Who Captures the Gains?

Where zoning loosens near transit, do residents benefit — or do developers absorb the gains through condo conversion?

Question 3

Ownership & Connectivity

Does corporate ownership predict transit connectivity — and if so, in which direction?

Datasets

This analysis uses three datasets from the provided MAPC collection, all within the A2 directory tree.

Primary · A2 Dataset
Single-Family Zoning vs. Other Housing, Metro Boston

Municipality-level zoning breakdown with census overlays. 101 municipalities.

housing_sf_other_w_census.*

GeoJSON of MBTA lines and stop locations with precomputed ½-mile buffer polygons.

Supplementary · Temporal

Neighborhood-level corporate vs. owner-occupant rates in Boston, 2004–2024.

CSV2004–2024
Corp_Ownership_and_Occupancy
_Over_Time.csv
02 — Analysis Questions & Motivations

Overall Questions

Question 1

Where is zoning most exclusionary across Metro Boston, and who lives in those places?

Motivation

Fischel's Zoning Rules! and MAPC's Exclusionary by Design describe how single-family zoning originated partly as an economic stratification tool — keeping out multi-family buildings effectively keeps out lower-income residents. Rothstein's The Color of Law documents how this was often racially motivated. I want to test whether this history shows up spatially today: do municipalities with the most single-family zoning also show higher incomes and lower racial diversity? As a student commuting through the Boston area, the abrupt shift in density and wealth at municipal lines — from Cambridge triple-deckers to Belmont single-family lots — makes this feel viscerally real, not just academic.

Fischel (2015) Zoning Rules!; Rothstein (2017) The Color of Law; MAPC (2020) Exclusionary by Design.
Question 2

Where zoning loosens near transit, who captures the gains — residents or developers?

Motivation

The MBTA Communities Act (2021) requires municipalities near transit to permit multi-family housing near stations, with the promise of greater affordability. But a well-documented critique — from Zuk et al. (2018) and others — is that upzoning in high-demand areas can trigger developer-driven gentrification: rental units get converted to condos, prices spike, and existing residents are priced out. The condo conversion dataset gives a direct window into whether developer activity clusters near transit, which would be consistent with capture rather than broad affordability gains.

Zuk et al. (2018) Gentrification, Displacement, and the Role of Public Investment; MBTA Communities Act guidance documents.
Question 3

Does corporate ownership predict how transit-connected a neighborhood is — and in which direction?

Motivation

This question is framed in a pointedly neutral manner. Existing work (Desmond 2016, Evicted) shows corporate landlords concentrate in specific neighborhoods, but the spatial logic is contested. Two plausible stories exist: (A) investors target transit-rich, high-demand neighborhoods; or (B) corporate landlords concentrate in transit-poor areas where residents have fewer exit options. High investor ownership could also coincide with areas that received road infrastructure investment, suggesting car-based rather than transit-based accessibility. Proximity to a stop alone is a poor proxy — a neighborhood 0.3 miles from an infrequent bus is less connected than one 0.8 miles from a Red Line station. So I used MBTA line type (heavy rail vs. bus) as a rough service-quality signal rather than raw distance.

Desmond (2016) Evicted; Been et al. (2019) Supply Skepticism: Housing Supply and Affordability; MAPC corporate ownership readings.
03 — Phase 1: Data Overview & Quality

Shape, Structure, and Data Quality

Before investigating questions, I checked each dataset's dimensions, distributions, and any quality issues that could affect analysis.

Geography mismatch: The corporate ownership data covers Boston neighborhoods and ZIPs, while the zoning data is at the municipality level. Joining these requires the Boston_Neighborhoods_Zipcodes.csv crosswalk; I detail data aggregation choices in the Q3 analysis.
04 — Phase 2: Discoveries & Insights

Discoveries & Insights

Q1

Where is zoning most exclusionary, and who lives there?

Start with the spatial distribution of exclusion, then ask whether income and racial composition follow the same geography by layering a second variable onto the map and testing the relationship in a scatter.

Q2

Where zoning loosens near transit, who captures the gains?

Spatially join condo conversion records to ½-mile MBTA stop buffers, then compare conversion density and price trajectories inside vs. outside those buffers. The key methodological move is a rate comparison (conversions per housing unit), not raw counts, to control for the fact that the transit-served inner city has more housing stock to begin with.

Q3

Does corporate ownership predict transit connectivity — and in which direction?

I use distance to the nearest rapid-transit stop (heavy rail or light rail) as the transit quality proxy, rather than raw distance to the nearest stop of any kind or GTFS headways. A neighborhood 0.3 miles from an infrequent commuter-rail station is less transit-connected than one 0.8 miles from the Orange Line. The 21 Boston neighborhoods in the corporate ownership dataset are classified into three tiers: Rapid Transit (≤ 0.8 km to the nearest rapid-transit stop, n = 13), Near Rapid Transit (0.8–1.5 km, n = 4), and Bus / CR Only (> 1.5 km, n = 4: Hyde Park, West Roxbury, Roslindale, South Boston).

05 — Summary

Main Lessons

Q1 — Finding

Exclusion is spatially concentrated in the outer ring as expected, but the correlation with income and race is weaker than the literature predicts: many wealthy municipalities have low SF zoning, and the 100%-SF category spans a wide income range. The bimodal distribution (many at 0%, many at 100%) is the most structurally important finding: there is little gradation, just two distinct zoning regimes.

Q2 — Finding

75% of all condo conversions (2016–2024) occur in rapid-transit-served ZIP codes, and those buildings carry a 50% higher median pre-conversion assessed value ($882k vs. $589k). The concentration is consistent with developer capture: transit access is being monetised through conversion activity, rather than unlocking affordable supply. The 2023 spike, when activity nearly tripled and the rail-ZIP share fell to 69%, hints that volume surges push developers into lower-cost bus-served areas.

Q3 — Finding

Story A holds: corporate ownership is strongly negatively correlated with distance to the nearest rapid-transit stop (r = −0.60). Transit-rich neighborhoods have ~27% corporate ownership vs. ~15% in bus/CR-only areas. All 4 bus-only neighborhoods sit at the bottom of the 2024 ranking. The fastest proportional growth (6.8×) is actually in bus-only areas — from a very low 2004 base — meaning corporate capital is spreading outward but the transit-proximity premium persists.

This is an initial exploration, not a comprehensive investigation. Key data decisions and limitations: (Q1) the census columns represent 1–2 sampled tracts per municipality rather than full municipal populations, which attenuates income and race correlations — correlations should be treated as directional indicators only; (Q2) boston_condo_conversions.csv lacks coordinates, so transit classification uses ZIP-code centroids against the ½-mile MBTA buffer polygons; prior-year assessed values are absent from 2022 records and nearly all 2023 records, limiting the value analysis to 2016, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, and 2024; (Q3) the Boston_Neighborhoods_Zipcodes.csv crosswalk does not include Fenway, North End, or West End under those names — these were manually mapped using known ZIP codes; neighborhood transit tiers are assigned by distance from a hand-coded geographic centroid to the nearest rapid-transit stop (GTFS types 0 and 1: light and heavy rail), not from official neighborhood boundary polygons. MBTA line type is used as the transit quality proxy rather than raw stop distance or schedule frequency.
